summary.brief: Knight v. Roberts. Medical malpractice case involving paediatrics.

evidence_index.text: Mrs. Knight was bathing her dog when she suddenly began experiencing a pain in her chest. Later that evening, Mrs. Knight went to TMC’s Emergency Department (“ER”), arriving at approximately 8:00 p.m.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Mrs. Knight reported that she was 61 years old, had a history of smoking and hypertension, and was then experiencing severe chest pain that radiated down her arm, back, and neck.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Roberts was the attending physician in the ER that evening, and he saw Mrs. Knight at 8:35 p.m., approximately 15 minutes after her initial assessment.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Roberts made a differential diagnosis of angina, myocardial infarction, pleurisy, costochondritis, esophageal reflux, and chest wall pain.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Mrs. Knight was not placed on a monitor until 9:20 p.m., and her medications were not given until 9:30 p.m., almost an hour after the orders were given.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Mrs. Knight was immediately transferred to TMC’s intensive care unit, and a cardiologist at St. Francis Hospital was consulted.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The catheterization performed on February 19 revealed that Mrs. Knight had an aortic dissection, a tear in the ascending aorta above her heart, which required emergency surgery.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Guyton opined that the surgery would have been difficult since she had been given blood thinners at TMC, which increased the risk of bleeding and the risk of mortality.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Robert A. Guyton

evidence_index.text: We conclude that the evidence presents a genuine issue of material fact as to whether the negligence of Dr. Roberts, Dr. Cone, and the nursing staff proximately caused Mrs. Knight’s death.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The trial court therefore did not err in denying Dr. Cone’s motion.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Knight’s experts, Dr. Coule and nurse Cathleen Provins Churbock, both attested that Mrs. Knight’s symptoms and condition required her to be triaged under the status of emergent pursuant to the standard of care.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Coule and Nurse Cathleen Provins Churbock

evidence_index.text: Dr. Coule testified that the required medications and treatment would have controlled Mrs. Knight’s blood pressure and the flow of blood going between the sections of her aorta.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Coule

evidence_index.text: The trial court was therefore authorized to conclude that she was qualified to serve as an expert in this case.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The issue of the admissibility or exclusion of expert testimony rests in the broad discretion of the court, and consequently, the trial court’s ruling thereon cannot be reversed absent an abuse of discretion.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Judgment reversed in Case No. A12A0740. Judgments affirmed in Case Nos. A12A0741 and A12A0770.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

initial_state.chief_complaint: Severe chest pain radiating down arm, back, and neck
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Mrs. Knight began experiencing chest pain while bathing her dog. She reported a pain level of 10/10.

focused_exam.chest: Pain radiating to arm, back, and neck

initial_state.initial_working_problem: Chest pain with differential including angina, myocardial infarction, pleurisy, costochondritis, esophageal reflux, and chest wall pain

reveal.decision_impact: Should prompt further investigation for other causes of chest pain

reveal.clinical_significance: Does not rule out aortic dissection
reveal.decision_impact: Should prompt consideration of further imaging

requestables.name: CT Scan of the Aorta
requestables.description: Computed tomography scan to assess for aortic dissection

timeline_phases.name: Emergency Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to ER with acute symptoms

key_events.event: Patient arrives at ER and is triaged

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient in ER with severe chest pain and hypertension
decision_points.prompt: What imaging investigation should be ordered?

options.description: Order CT scan of the aorta
options.clinical_reasoning: CT scan is necessary to diagnose aortic dissection
Chest x-ray does not rule out aortic dissection

actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Defendant did not consider aortic dissection

expected_action_court.description: CT scan should have been ordered
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated CT scan was necessary

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to order CT scan delayed diagnosis
explanation.what_should_have_happened: CT scan would have confirmed aortic dissection

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed aortic dissection leading to myocardial infarction and death

key_findings: Delay in treatment contributed to death

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: CT would have shown aortic dissection

treatments.outcome: Not performed due to high risk

court_findings.finding: Dr. Roberts breached duty of care
